headline: Lewis and Clark Trail Commission Established, 1964
body:
On the sixth day of October, in the year of our Lord one thousand nine hundred and sixty-four, a significant decree emerged from the hallowed halls of the United States Congress, bearing the imprimatur of President Lyndon B. Johnson. This was Public Law 88-630, an act not of conquest or conflict, but of commemoration and conservation, establishing with due solemnity the Lewis and Clark Trail Commission. Its purpose, as articulated within the very fabric of the law, was to cast a guiding light upon the epochal journey undertaken by Captains Meriwether Lewis and William Clark, those intrepid pathfinders who, in the early dawn of the nineteenth century, had charted a course from the confluence of the Missouri to the vast, untamed Pacific Northwest.

The spirit of this Commission was born from a desire to etch into the national consciousness the profound significance of that expedition, a venture that had, with every stroke of the oar and every weary step, unveiled the boundless grandeur of a nascent nation. It sought not merely to mark a trail upon the earth, but to illuminate the very essence of exploration, the relentless pursuit of knowledge, and the enduring human spirit in the face of an immense, formidable wilderness. The Commission was tasked with the identification, the careful marking, and the perpetual preservation of this historic route, ensuring its availability for the edification and enjoyment of generations yet unborn.

Beyond the mere tracing of a path, the Act harbored a deeper ambition: to foster a profound appreciation for the colossal resources of the sprawling region through which Lewis and Clark had journeyed. This appreciation, it was hoped, would in turn cultivate desirable long-term conservation objectives, safeguarding the public interest of both the region's inhabitants and the nation at large. It was a recognition that the wild, untrammeled beauty encountered by the Corps of Discovery was a heritage to be cherished, a testament to the natural munificence of the American continent. The Commission, though advisory in nature, was empowered to make recommendations to federal and state agencies, as well as to private entities, encouraging the development of a suitable network of roads, adorned with appropriate markers, that would follow the general course of the historic trail.

The composition of this august body reflected the vast geographical and political scope of the expedition itself. It was to be a gathering of twenty-seven souls, drawn from the ten states traversed by the original explorers—Missouri, Kansas, Iowa, Nebraska, South Dakota, North Dakota, Montana, Idaho, Washington, and Oregon—each represented by its Governor or a designated proxy. Further, it included four members from the House of Representatives, four from the Senate, and five Secretaries, or their representatives, from the Departments of Interior, Agriculture, Defense, Health, Education, and Welfare, and Commerce. To these were added four members appointed by the J. N. "Ding" Darling Foundation, a testament to the broad coalition of interests united in this noble endeavor. The Commission, though destined to cease its existence after five years, laid the groundwork for a lasting legacy, ensuring that the epic journey of Lewis and Clark, and the wilderness they traversed, would forever remain a vibrant chapter in the American story. The echoes of their footsteps, once faint upon the vast plains and towering mountains, were now to be amplified, their journey a perpetual source of inspiration and a call to stewardship for the land they so meticulously observed.
